data.source: Laboratory experiments with undergraduate student participants providing ratings and evaluations via questionnaires
data.sample_size: Study 1a: 132; Study 1b: 206; Study 1c: 139; Study 2a: 117; Study 2b: 72; Study 3: 54; Study 4: 102
data.unit_of_analysis: Individual participant responses (e.g., product attitude function ratings, purchase likelihood, advertisement evaluations)
data.notes: Multiple studies used utilitarian vs. symbolic product categories and brand-level vs. category-level framings; appeals were designed to be function-matching or mismatching; some studies used real brands (e.g., Bounty, Champion), others used fictitious brands; order was counterbalanced; measures included benefit and symbolism ratings and their difference scores.

method.description: A series of controlled lab experiments examined whether branding alters the attitude functions (utilitarian vs. symbolic) associated with products and whether this shift attenuates the advantage of function-matching persuasive appeals. Participants rated products or brands on benefit vs. symbolism, evaluated persuasive appeals at brand vs. category levels, and assessed advertisements. Analyses included mixed ANOVAs, t-tests, and regressions.
steps: Select products commonly associated with predominantly utilitarian or symbolic attitudes at the category level (e.g., paper towels vs. college T-shirts).

Randomly assign participants to evaluate products at the category level or the brand level; in some studies, specify real or fictitious brand names; in one study, use a 'new brand' framing without a specific name.
Measure attitude functions using items assessing the extent participants think of the product in terms of concrete benefits vs. what it symbolizes; compute difference scores (benefits − symbolism).

For persuasion studies, present either utilitarian (benefit-focused) or symbolic (value/symbolism-focused) appeals at the category or brand level; collect purchase likelihood or ad evaluation ratings.
In Study 3, also measure each participant’s idiosyncratic category-level attitude function and test whether appeal effectiveness depends on function-match at the category vs. brand level.

In Study 4, use real magazine advertisements; rate each ad’s focus on benefits vs. symbols; separately assess category-level functions for the advertised product categories; compute mismatch between category function and ad function; correlate mismatch with ad effectiveness ratings.

Analyze data using mixed ANOVAs (factors include level: brand vs. category; product type: utilitarian vs. symbolic; appeal type: matching vs. mismatching), t-tests, and regressions including interaction terms.
method.models: Mixed ANOVA (level × product type; level × appeal; product factors), independent-samples t-tests, multiple regression with interaction terms (including three-way interactions), correlations
method.outcome_variable: Attitude function ratings (benefits and symbolism, and their difference), purchase likelihood, advertisement effectiveness evaluations
method.independent_variables: Branding level (brand vs. category), product type (utilitarian vs. symbolic), appeal type (utilitarian vs. symbolic; matching vs. mismatching the category-level function), brand identity (real vs. fictitious), individual attitude function (participant-specific difference score)
method.control_variables: Product fixed effects and counterbalanced order; in some studies, brand name sets balanced; analyses included product factors within ANOVAs

results.summary: Across multiple studies, branding systematically shifted attitude functions: utilitarian product categories became relatively more symbolic at the brand level, while symbolic categories became more utilitarian. Consequently, the well-documented advantage of function-matching appeals at the category level was attenuated or eliminated at the brand level, and sometimes reversed. Individual differences in category-level attitude functions moderated appeal effectiveness at the category level but not at the brand level. In a study of real advertisements, greater mismatch between ad function and category function was associated with more favorable evaluations for branded products.

metadata.original_paper_title: Branding Alters Attitude Functions and Reduces the Advantage of Function-Matching Persuasive Appeals
